title: The Birds Who Sought the King — ʿAṭṭār and the Imaginal Journey to Qaf
author: Farīd ud-Dīn ʿAṭṭār

body.text: Every bird of the world gathers because something is missing — wings, songs, colours, habits, excuses — but no king. The soul is a flock, each faculty a bird with a reason to travel. ʿAṭṭār's poem places the whole human being on stage as a field of figures.

Once on a time from all the Circles seven, 'Twixt earth and sky the Birds their counsel had, Held their assembly and a Grand Divan, Public and private, to appoint a King. They said among themselves: "Since our affairs, Of which we are the heirs and principals, Have neither King nor Head nor Governance, Our scattered state will fall into decay. Some one assume the sovereignty, and be Our common Refuge in perplexity."

Birds are psychic forms — pride, fear, love of comfort, each speaking from a limitation. The Hoopoe sees the disorder and points toward the hidden king — the inner guide who knows the way. Qaf: the edge of ordinary orientation, the same geography as Corbin's eighth climate — the threshold where the soul's states become landscapes.

body.caption: The soul as a parliament — every faculty gathered, seeking its lost centre.

body.text: The birds, according to the poet, were tired of their interminable anarchy and desired to choose a king. Their wise men told them that they had a king, the Simurgh, who lived in the distant mountains of Kaf, but many obstacles must be overcome before they could come into his presence. A long and terrible journey must be undertaken. Some of the birds refused the journey, and were excused after telling the story of their refusal. The others set out, and after endless adventures, their number was reduced to thirty.

The king is found by transformation. Each bird exposes an excuse, the Hoopoe answers — the excuse becomes a mirror. Journey to Qaf is a method of reduction. Excuses burn off. False identities fall. The many gather until the hidden unity can look back through them.
This celebrated Sufi poem, also known as Conference of the Birds, is a journey to the world mountain Qaf. Each bird has a particular significance, a special fault, and a tale to tell.

Thirty birds arrive. Sī murgh — 'thirty birds.' The king appears through the purified remnant of the seekers themselves. This is imaginal logic: the symbol is an external object to be possessed. It is a transformation in the structure of seeing.

body.caption: Thirty birds before the Sīmurgh — the seeker becoming what it sought.

body.text: The path to Qaf burns away what is false. The flock becomes a single seeing.
